Okay, so reference checking guidelines. So say you've gone past the phone screen, you've conducted an interview, you've identified the other skills, now you want to check the references to make sure that there's nothing that, you know, there's no information presented to you then that could be a red flag, right? So I want to give you some guidelines for this. One, you want to remember that you are extending trust when you hire someone, meaning if they are hired, you should already trust them. And so that's the kind of expectations you as the business owner need to take on, which means you do not hire lightly, right? You need to hire with the intention of trusting them as soon as they come on. So you're not like tiptoeing around the fact that they're new, right? And so if someone passes all the tests, then it's time to gather some social proof for them, right? This means you actually call their references. Like a lot of people look at that and then they just like pass through it because it takes work to call references. But reference checks are by far the most important stuff in making sure that you're not about to bring on someone who you will very soon need to let go. And I can tell you from personal experience, this is incredibly important to do, okay? Now I need to let you know a disclaimer. I am not lawyers and not legal advice, but I'm letting you know you are required by law to ask the same questions to every single candidate's references. This is actually a legal obligation of yours, meaning if you have Sally, you have to ask Sally's references the exact same three questions that you ask Jim's references, okay? So just remember that as you're going through this process. Guidelines. So make sure that the candidate is aware that they need to provide references and accurate references to their past employers, okay? So make sure that they are aware that you are going to actually be using these references. Two, this should be done after the candidate makes it through at least two rounds of interviewing, right? And that's prior to hiring. So you don't want to tell someone they have the job and then call the references. You also don't want to call references after one interview if you don't know if they have the skills yet, right? So you want to make sure you do it after you know they have the skills before you hire them. Three, do not take the easy way out and shoot an email. You want to make sure that you call references. Why is it important that you call? Because tone and inflexuation voiced will tell you a lot about the answers they're giving you. Emails are much harder to interpret, okay? And lastly, don't skip out on this just because they have like passed the test so far and you've got great feeling about them. Like just because you like someone does not mean that they're the best fit for you or for your culture, okay? So it's really important that you take these precautions. So some example questions that we've provided as well to you. One, can you describe the candidates' work performance, right? Did they exceed expectations? They meet expectations. Were they below expectations, right? Two, was the candidate accountable in performing tasks, right? So did they meet deadlines? Did they need someone to check up on them or were they self-sufficient, right? Three, what are the candidates' strengths and weaknesses? This is a great one to hear. And then four, company policies aside. The biggest one, would you rehire the candidate? Okay? I always look for someone that says yes. So that being said, that is how you do reference checks and some examples for things to look out for and questions to ask.
